note:
昆士兰的一群海豚一直在从海里带着礼物上岸，显然是因为它们缺少与人类的互动。
座头海豚通常在阳光海岸以北的锡罐湾的藤壶咖啡馆和海豚一起与游客打成一片。
但由于冠状病毒的限制，游客已经几周没有排队喂食这些动物了。
海洋生物提供的珍宝包括海绵、覆盖藤壶的瓶子和珊瑚碎片。
咖啡馆Facebook页面上的一篇帖子写道：“这个豆荚一直在给我们带来常规礼物，告诉我们他们有多想念公众的互动和关注。”
一名志愿者告诉美国广播公司，虽然豆荚以前也表现出了这种行为，但随着场馆的关闭，这种行为有所增加。
海豚行为专家巴里·麦戈文(Barry McGoven)表示，海豚送礼可能是因为它们错过了人类，但可能性不大。
“海豚和它们的行为再也不会让我感到惊讶了，”密歇根大学博士生麦戈文告诉7NEWS.com.au。
“他们什么都做--他们使用工具，他们有文化，他们有一些与招牌口哨中的名字相似的东西。
“很可能，它们本身并不怀念人类。他们可能会错过一顿免费的饭菜和例行公事。“。
他说：“他们经常玩弄杂草、珊瑚和各种各样的东西，然后就把它们放在主席台(鼻子)上。”
“它们现在已经习惯了进食，所以它们习惯了人类的到来。
“当它没有发生的时候，也许只是出于无聊。”
note_en:
A pod of dolphins in Queensland has been bringing ashore gifts from the sea - apparently because they’re missing interaction with humans.
 The humpback dolphins usually mingle with visitors at Barnacles Cafe & Dolphin Feeding at Tin Can Bay, north of the Sunshine Coast.
 But it’s been weeks since visitors lined up to feed the animals due to coronavirus restrictions.
  Among the treasures the marine creatures have provided are sea sponges, barnacle-encrusted bottles and pieces of coral.
  “The pod has been bringing us regular gifts, showing us how much they’re missing the public interaction and attention,” said a post on the cafe’s Facebook page.
  A volunteer told the ABC while the pod has displayed the behaviour before, it had increased while the venue was closed.
   Barry McGovern, an expert in dolphin behaviour, said it was possible the dolphins were giving gifts because they missed humans, but unlikely.
 “Nothing surprises me with dolphins and their behaviour anymore,” McGovern, a UQ PhD student, told 7NEWS.com.au.
 “They do everything - they use tools, they have culture, they have something similar to names in signature whistles.
 “In all likelihood, they probably don’t miss humans per se. They probably miss a free meal and the routine.”
   “They often play with bits of weed and coral and all sorts of things and just leave it on their rostrum (nose),” he said.
 “They’re used to getting fed now, so they’re used to humans coming in.
 “When it’s not happening, maybe it’s just out of boredom.”
title: 热门景点的海豚错过了游客，不断在岸上留下“礼物”
title_en: Dolphins at popular spot miss tourists and keep leaving 'gifts' on shore

note:
马克·扎克伯格(Mark Zuckerberg)计划将Facebook Inc.。在接下来的十年里，朝着远程劳动力的方向发展，永久地重新配置这家科技巨头围绕冠状病毒大流行强加给它的分散结构的运营。
这位Facebook首席执行官周四向员工概述了这项计划，这是商界领袖承诺扩大公司迅速接受的做法以适应危机的最引人注目的例子之一。在从工作场所到战略的问题上，经理们正在重新考虑……

Mark Zuckerberg plans to shift Facebook Inc. toward a substantially remote workforce over the next decade, permanently reconfiguring the tech giant’s operations around the dispersed structure that the coronavirus pandemic forced on it.
  The plan, which the Facebook chief executive outlined to employees on Thursday, is one of the highest-profile examples of business leaders committing to extend the practices their companies quickly embraced to adjust to the crisis. On matters from workplace to strategy, managers are rethinking...
title: 在冠状病毒之后，Facebook将永久转向更远程的工作
title_en: Facebook to Shift Permanently Toward More Remote Work After Coronavirus

note:
当开发商金格·希茨克(Ginger Hitzke)第一次提出在索拉纳海滩(Solana Beach)的一个停车场上建造一个经济适用房综合体时，她设想为低收入家庭和成年人建造18套新住房，每套公寓的成本为41.4万美元。
十多年后，她的项目规模缩小了近一半，成本增加了一倍多。
珍珠公寓的每套公寓价格为110万美元，是该州最昂贵的保障性住房项目，可能也是全国最昂贵的保障性住房项目。这也是一个令人震惊的例子，说明在越来越多的加州人需要住房的时候，政治、经济和官僚力量是如何汇聚在一起，推高此类住房的成本的。
加州在为低收入居民建造政府补贴公寓楼的成本方面处于全国领先地位。“纽约时报”对州政府数据的分析发现，公寓的平均价格约为50万美元。在过去的十年中，经通胀调整后的价格上涨了26%。
臭名昭著的高地价和不断上涨的建筑材料成本是部分原因。但泰晤士报发现，州和地方政府控制范围内的众多因素也是罪魁祸首，包括邻居的反对，以及迫使开发商满足劳工和环境标准的规定，这些标准往往超过豪华公寓的要求。
所有这些都使加州缓解无家可归和负担得起的住房危机的努力复杂化，原因是低收入家庭缺少130万套住房，天价租金和与之相匹配的贫困率。
由于冠状病毒大流行已经导致大量失业，特别是在低薪的酒店业和服务业，专家认为，对经济适用房的需求只会增长。经济破坏也可能严重抑制政府税收，使可用于资助新建设的资金减少。
加州大学伯克利分校特纳住房创新中心(Terner Center For Housing Innovation)的教师研究顾问卡罗琳娜·里德(Carolina Reid)表示，这加大了加州全面改革低收入住房建设方式的压力。里德是一项关于低收入住房建设成本的新研究的作者。
里德说：“从城市到开发商再到建筑工人，片中的每一个演员都将在未来几年面临冠状病毒的压力。”“这场公共卫生危机给我们已经需要的改革增加了更多的紧迫性。”
近年来，加州各地的选民已经批准了数十亿美元为低收入家庭和无家可归者建造住房。但这些计划往往未能达到公众的预期，因为这数十亿美元建造的公寓越来越少。
除了珍珠酒店，时报还发现加州的六个保障性住房开发项目-都位于旧金山湾区-也已经超过了每套公寓的建造费用90万美元。其中一半在去年开业，其余的正在建设中。
建筑成本也在远离海岸的地方飙升。帝国郡是该州最贫穷的县之一，在过去的十年里，这一成本攀升了70%。在弗雷斯诺县，经济适用房的平均价格翻了一番，达到每套37.6万美元，比中位数的独户住宅高出三分之一。
事实上，加州大学伯克利分校(UC Berkeley)的另一项研究发现，加州开发商为建造经济适用房所支付的费用，高于他们为更广泛的人群建造市价住房所支付的费用。根据美国政府问责局最近的一份报告，加州是美国建筑成本最高的地方，该报告将加州与纽约市和其他10个城市和州进行了比较。时报发现，如果这里的建筑和其他地方一样便宜，2011年至2015年间，可能会有大约1.2万个低收入家庭获得住房。
为什么这些房子的建造成本如此之高？原因之一是法规要求开发商支付建筑工人工会级别的工资。另一个是加州错综复杂的融资过程，这迫使开发商在12个不同的融资项目中导航，这些项目由五个独立的部门控制，权力由州长和州财政部长划分。
开发商还必须安抚地方政府，地方政府经常通过坚持减少公寓和增加停车位来降低项目的经济可行性。这种情况发生在富裕的圣地亚哥县飞地索拉纳海滩(Solana Beach)，珍珠酒店在那里从18套公寓削减到10套，批准需要一个有53个停车位的地下车库。
累积的结果是，数百万加州人无法找到负担得起的住所。
珍珠号的传奇故事始于30年前索拉纳海滩一家破旧的汽车旅馆。米格尔·扎莫拉(Miguel Zamora)与妻子和四个孩子住在汽车旅馆里，他记得热水来来去去，屋顶漏水，厕所泛滥。房间里到处都是蟑螂、跳蚤和老鼠。
萨莫拉在建筑业工作，是一名洗碗工和园丁，他说：“一切都是泥土。”“它甚至还没有铺好。”
1992年，索拉纳海滩市政府对该汽车旅馆位于比佛利山的老板提起刑事诉讼。作为和解的一部分，该市拆除了汽车旅馆，但同意在1999年之前为萨莫拉的家人和其他六名居民提供新的负担得起的住房。
萨莫拉和其他人还收到了联邦住房援助券，这帮助他在距离旧汽车旅馆5英里的地方找到了一套公寓。他的家人在过去的20年里一直住在那里，但他们从未收到的公寓仍然压在他的心头。
如果珍珠酒店或其他低收入住房在这座城市建成，萨莫拉有权首先搬进来。他渴望将他的大家庭聚集在一个更有家的感觉的地方。
67岁的萨莫拉说：“我想要享受我的孙子孙女。”“因为分开很难。”
尽管提供经济适用房的最后期限是1999年，但索拉纳海滩的领导人又花了近10年的时间才迈出了要求开发商推销项目的第一步。
希兹克提议在一个2万平方英尺的公共停车场上建造珍珠酒店，该停车场位于旧汽车旅馆所在街区的悬崖边，还有一个海滩接入点。这18套公寓包括足够的三居室和四居室单元，可以容纳萨莫拉的家人和其他有权获得新住房的人。
居民和公众将支付地下车库的停车费，这将有助于为该项目提供资金，就像从一家小杂货店租用一样。珍珠酒店每套公寓的建造成本为41.4万美元。
市政府官员很感兴趣，但知道这个想法会给索拉纳海滩的1.3万名居民带来问题。这个社区的家庭年收入中位数超过10万美元。停车场坐落在黄金地段，由白色围栏与分时租赁隔开。穿过一条绿树成荫的街道，是一座三层的共管公寓综合体，那里的一套两居室单元最近以120万美元的价格售出。
在与市政府进行了一年的谈判后，希兹克同意将珍珠酒店缩小到10套公寓，并允许公众免费停车在这个53个停车位的车库里。重新设计将该项目的价格提高到每套公寓略低于60万美元。
在这一点上，2010年，希兹克和市政府官员准备向索拉纳海滩的居民揭开珍珠的面纱。事情进行得不太顺利。
“低收入人群倾向于拥有年久失修的汽车和骑摩托车，这增加了晚上8点熄灯的噪音。”邻近的共管公寓业主玛丽莲·里纳尔迪(Marylyn Rinaldi)在2011年写给市议会的一封信中写道，这封信后来在一起针对该项目的诉讼中被引用。
Rinaldi和其他人说，经济适用房不属于他们的社区，并充斥着议会的抱怨。
绝大多数住房是由政府补贴的。事实上，在加州和美国各地，低收入租房者的收入远远低于房主，房主可以对抵押贷款支付的利息进行减税。
尽管如此，联邦和州政府每年仍在负担得起的租赁住房上花费数十亿美元。它的主要融资方式是税收抵免，这将减少银行和其他大型投资者在帮助支付住房项目时欠美国国税局和国库的金额。在洛杉矶，一个四口之家的年收入必须不超过62,640美元，才有资格获得为收入低于该地区收入中位数60%的人建造的住房。反过来，该家庭每月最多支付1410美元购买一套两居室公寓。
许多大州只有一个部门发放经济适用房资金。加州有五个。这些部门向不同的民选官员报告，没有人负责监督整个系统。
州长加文·纽瑟姆(Gavin Newsom)以艰辛的方式吸取了这一教训。去年，他发布了一项行政命令，旨在通过提供国有土地进行开发，加快建设，降低保障性住房的成本。
首批利用的项目之一是提议在萨克拉门托市中心建立56个小工作室。但两个月前，一个为经济适用房提供债券融资的部门-一个由财政部控制的部门，而不是纽瑟姆-拒绝了对该项目的资助。它现在处于不确定状态。
开发商必须提交一系列申请才能获得十几个不同的融资项目，这些项目就像债券融资部门一样，有自己的法规来决定哪些项目获胜。更令人困惑的是，有三个独立的项目来资助无家可归者的住房，其中四个是针对公共交通附近的经济适用房-每个项目都有不同的规则。
为了赢得州政府的资金，开发商往往首先必须从当地运营的保障性住房项目和债券措施中获得资金-比如洛杉矶的HHH无家可归者住房债券提案-这些项目都有自己的规定。
对于开发商来说，拼凑多个收入来源的过程代价高昂。泰晤士报发现，每增加一个资金来源，每个单位的平均成本就会增加6000美元以上。
融资需求的数量导致开发商聘请顾问、律师和合规官员。美国GAO的研究发现，加州保障性住房项目14%的价格由这些类型的行政成本组成-这是全国最高的，超过了开发商在土地上的支出。
加州住房伙伴关系公司(California Housing Partnership Corp.)总裁马特·施瓦茨(Matt Schwartz)说，因此，项目积压了很多。该公司帮助开发商获得融资。
施瓦茨说：“我们办公室里所有这些应用程序的工作人员都快疯了。”“我们跟不上工作量。”
该州不堪重负，以至于在去年夏天的一次公开会议上，财政部的一名高级代表承认，他最近发现办公室里堆放着成堆的文件，因为工作人员无法足够快地处理这些文件。
纽瑟姆承诺今年将领导一项努力，简化开发商获得州政府资金购买保障性住房的方式。
“我受够了TCAC和CDLAC、OPR、CalVet、HCD和CalHFA，”这位州长说，并在1月份公布他的预算提案时，提到了参与融资项目的部门的名字。“你们六个人听得懂我刚才说的话。其他人都不知道。这就是问题所在。“。
2014年春季，索拉纳海滩市议会在长达四个小时的会议结束时最终批准了珍珠号。
几乎立刻，街对面的公寓建筑群协会就提起诉讼，辩称该市不能在这块土地上建造住房，因为地契要求它必须是停车场。共管公寓协会输掉了官司和上诉，但花了两年半的时间才解决。
之后，希茨克得以从州政府获得一些税收抵免，但法律上的拖延给她带来了一个新的挑战：建筑成本。
自该项目获批以来的六年里，每套公寓价格上涨了62%，她几乎将其全部归因于劳动力和材料。总体而言，加州大学伯克利分校的研究发现，在此期间，加州保障性住房项目的成本上涨了30%。
由于每套公寓的建设成本为110万美元，希兹克一直无法找到足够的州和地方资金来破土动工。州和地方政府拒绝给她仍然需要的100万美元。邻居们从未停止过与开发项目的斗争。
欧内斯特·库尔夏特(Ernest Kurschat)是明珠酒店提议选址旁边的一家分时度假酒店的居民，他在2018年写信反对该项目，猜测大楼内的零售空间将成为“低收入住房的食品券办公室”。
索拉纳海滩的领导人继续支持珍珠酒店，然而，他们认为，在一个房屋价值中值为140万美元的社区，大部分建筑成本是不可避免的。
希兹克说，如果不包括大型地下停车库，她将有足够的资金建设这个开发项目，它距离一个主要的通勤火车站不到一英里。
但市议员大卫·齐托(David Zito)为车库进行了辩护。他说，即使索拉纳海滩在法律上被允许取消停车，这座城市也“在道德上、道义上有义务”因为“居住和谐”而更换它。这与他认为珍珠的高度应该与周围社区相适应的原因是相同的-这样它就不会让人想起臭名昭著的高层公共住房。
“你开始谈论完全负担得起的大项目，人们开始考虑‘项目’，对吗？就像芝加哥的卡布里尼-格林(Cabrini-Green)之类的，“齐托说，他是唯一一位投票批准珍珠号的民选官员，目前仍在议会任职。“然后，这就变成了一场完全不同的讨论。”
去年，旧金山湾区城市阿拉米达开设了Everett Commons，这是一个低收入开发项目，为20个家庭提供住房，每套公寓的建造成本为94.7万美元。该项目没有面临与珍珠号同等程度的反对。但在Everett Commons批准之前的十年里，拟建公寓的数量从36套减少到了20套。
约翰·诺克斯·怀特(John Knox White)在计划委员会任职期间曾投票支持埃弗雷特·康姆斯(Everett Commons)，现在是市议会议员。他说，他对该市对被迫离开阿拉米达的低收入家庭重视程度太低表示遗憾。
他说，这是一个“为与政治进程有关的人提供了过多和不适当的发言权和影响力”的制度。“谁在说话，谁在听，都很倾斜，而且偏向有土地、有财产的人.”
即使州官员能够精简官僚机构，地方官员能够抵御邻居的反对，加州高房价的其他驱动因素仍然与民主党关键选民所支持的目标有关：工会和环保人士。
许多州和地方的资助项目要求低收入住房开发商向建筑工人支付工会水平的工资。例如，在洛杉矶，这些规定要求开发商支付水管工每小时51美元的工资，而该地区的水管工每小时工资中位数为24美元。
加州大学伯克利分校的报告发现，支付工会水平工资的项目每套公寓的成本可能会增加5万美元。“纽约时报”的一项分析发现了类似的趋势。
加州劳工领袖引用了其他研究，包括他们自己的研究，结论是成本影响要低得多。除此之外，他们认为建筑工人在建造低收入住房时应该赚足够的钱，这样他们自己就不需要低收入住房了。州议员经常重复这一论点。
为了有最好的机会赢得税收抵免资金，低收入住房开发商还必须将他们的项目建设到超过国家对新建豪华公寓开发商的要求的环境标准。这包括他们的大部分电力使用太阳能，或者向LEED或其他第三方认证他们的能效。
非营利开发商Mercy Housing的高级副总裁道格·舒梅克(Doug Shoemaker)表示，国家资助的经济适用房应该是“世界上最优越、最节能的建筑”。“我不确定最后一点好处是否真的值得为此付出代价。”
加州大学伯克利分校的研究发现，按照更严格的环境标准建造的项目，每套公寓的成本比不符合标准的项目高出1.7万美元。
珍珠的资金要求要求该建筑获得LEED认证，并要求Hitzke为建筑工人支付工会水平的工资。最初的预算留出了盈利的空间。但早在2013年，希茨克就意识到她不会从这笔交易中赚到任何钱。
去年12月，该州通知她，由于该项目尚未破土动工，计划取消对她的资助。一个月前，希茨克会见了市政府官员，告诉他们她想退出她的开发协议。
希兹克说：“我很高兴能做完这件事。”“它除了杀了我什么也没做。但同时，我也尽我所能把这件事做好。“。
与此同时，萨莫拉和他的家人仍在五英里外的公寓里等待。这些天，当他开车经过他的旧社区时，他只感到失望。

When developer Ginger Hitzke first proposed an affordable housing complex on a parking lot in Solana Beach, she envisioned building 18 new homes for low-income families and adults at a cost of $414,000 per apartment.
 More than a decade later, her project has shrunk in size by nearly half and become more than twice as expensive.
 At $1.1 million per apartment, the Pearl is the priciest affordable housing project in the state and, likely, the country. It also serves as an alarming example of how political, economic and bureaucratic forces have converged to drive up the cost of such housing at a time when growing numbers of Californians need it.
      California leads the nation in the cost of building government-subsidized apartment complexes for low-income residents. A Times analysis of state data found that apartments cost an average of about $500,000. In the last decade, the price tag has grown 26%, after adjusting for inflation.
 The notoriously high price of land and the rising cost of construction materials are part of the reason. But The Times found that numerous factors within the control of state and local governments also are to blame, including opposition from neighbors and rules that compel developers to meet labor and environmental standards that often exceed what’s required for luxury condominiums.
 All this has complicated California’s efforts to alleviate its homelessness and affordable housing crises, driven by a shortage of 1.3 million homes for low-income households, sky-high rental prices and a poverty rate to match.
 With the coronavirus pandemic already  leading to significant job losses, particularly in the low-wage hospitality and service industries,   experts believe the demand for affordable housing is only going to grow. The economic damage also is likely to severely depress government tax revenue, leaving less money available to finance new construction.
 That steps up pressure on California to overhaul the way it builds low-income housing, said Carolina Reid, faculty research advisor at UC Berkeley’s Terner Center for Housing Innovation and author of  a new study on the cost to build low-income housing.
  “Every single one of the actors in it, from cities to developers to construction workers, is going to face stress from the coronavirus for years,” Reid said. “This public health crisis adds more urgency to making the reforms we had already needed.”
 In recent years, voters across California have approved billions of dollars to build homes for low-income families and homeless people. But those plans  often have failed to meet public expectations because those billions build fewer and fewer apartments.
  In addition to the Pearl, The Times found  six affordable housing developments in California — all in the Bay Area — that have eclipsed $900,000 per apartment to build as well. Half opened in the last year and the rest are under construction.
 Building costs have soared far from the coast as well. In Imperial County, one of the state’s poorest, the cost has climbed 70% in the last decade. And in Fresno County, the average price for affordable housing has doubled to $376,000 per apartment, an amount a third higher than a median single-family home.
 Indeed, what developers in California pay to build affordable housing is more than what they do for market-rate homes for the broader population,  another UC Berkeley study found. California was the most expensive place to build in the country, according to  a recent report  from the U.S. Government Accountability Office, which compared the state to New York City and 10 other cities and states. If building was as cheap here as everywhere else, about 12,000 more low-income families could have received homes between 2011 and 2015, The Times found.
 Why do these homes cost so much to build? One reason is that regulations require developers to pay construction workers union-level wages. Another is California’s labyrinthine financing process, which forces developers to navigate a dozen different funding programs controlled by five separate departments with authority divided between the governor and state treasurer.
 Developers also have to appease local governments, which often make projects less economically viable by insisting on fewer apartments and more parking. That happened in the wealthy San Diego County enclave of Solana Beach, where the Pearl was cut from 18 apartments to 10 and its approval required an underground garage with 53 parking spots.
 The cumulative result is that millions of Californians have been unable to find an affordable place to live.
  The Pearl’s saga began three decades ago in a rundown motel in Solana Beach. Miguel Zamora, who lived in the motel with his wife and four children, remembers that the hot water came and went, the roof leaked and toilets overflowed. Cockroaches, fleas and rats infested the rooms.
  “It was all dirt,” said Zamora, who worked in construction and as a dishwasher and gardener. “It wasn’t even paved.”
  In 1992, the city of Solana Beach filed a criminal complaint against the motel’s Beverly Hills-based owner. As part of the settlement, the city demolished the motel, but agreed to provide Zamora’s family and more than half a dozen other residents new affordable housing by 1999.
 Zamora and the others also received federal housing assistance vouchers, which helped him find an apartment five miles away from the old motel. His family has been living there for the last two decades, but the apartment they never received still weighs on his mind.
 Should the Pearl or other low-income housing ever get built in the city, Zamora has the right to move in first. He longs to bring his extended family together in a place that feels more like home.
 “I’d like to enjoy my grandchildren,” said Zamora, 67. “Because being apart is hard.”
 Even though the deadline to provide the affordable housing was 1999, it took almost another 10 years for Solana Beach’s leaders to take the first step of asking developers to pitch projects.
  Hitzke proposed building the Pearl on a 20,000-square-foot public parking lot, situated along a bluff on the same block as the old motel and a beach access point. The 18 apartments included enough three- and four-bedroom units to accommodate Zamora’s family and the others entitled to new housing.
 Residents and the public would pay for parking in an underground garage, which would help finance the project, as would rent from a small grocery store. The Pearl would cost $414,000 per apartment to build.
  City officials were intrigued but knew the idea would cause problems among Solana Beach’s 13,000 residents. The community has a median annual household income of more than $100,000. The parking lot sits on prime real estate, separated by a white fence from timeshare rentals. Across a tree-lined street is a three-story condominium complex where a two-bedroom unit recently sold for $1.2 million.
  After a year of negotiations with the city, Hitzke agreed to shrink the Pearl to 10 apartments and make it free for the public to park in the 53-space garage. The redesign raised the price of the project to just under $600,000 per apartment.
 At that point, in 2010, Hitzke and city officials were ready to unveil the Pearl to Solana Beach’s residents. It didn’t go well.
 “Low-income people tend to own cars that are in disrepair and ride motorcycles adding to the noise of a ‘lights out at 8 p.m. community,’” Marylyn Rinaldi, a neighboring condominium owner, wrote in 2011 in a letter to the City Council that was later cited in a lawsuit over the project.
 Rinaldi and others said that affordable housing didn’t belong in their community, and flooded the council with complaints.
  The vast majority of housing is subsidized by the government. In fact, in California and across the U.S., low-income renters receive far less than homeowners, who can take a tax deduction for interest paid on their mortgages.
 Still, each year federal and state governments spend billions of dollars on affordable rental housing. The primary way it’s funded is through tax credits, which reduce what banks and other large investors owe the Internal Revenue Service and state treasury if they help pay for housing projects. In Los Angeles, a family of four must make no more than $62,640 a year to qualify for housing built for those earning less than 60% of the region’s median income. In turn, that family would pay a maximum of $1,410 a month for a two-bedroom apartment.
  Many large states have only one department that hands out affordable housing funding. California has five. And those departments report to different elected officials, leaving no one in charge of overseeing the system as a whole.
  Gov. Gavin Newsom has learned this lesson the hard way. Last year,  he issued an executive order that aimed to speed up construction and reduce the cost of affordable housing by offering state-owned land for development.
 One of the first projects  to take advantage was a proposal for 56 small studios in downtown Sacramento. But two months ago, a department that hands out bond financing for affordable housing — one controlled by the Treasurer’s Office, not Newsom — rejected funding for the project. It’s now in limbo.
 Developers have to file a series of applications to access a dozen different funding programs that, like the bond financing department, have their own regulations determining what projects win. To make matters more confusing, there are three separate programs to finance homeless housing, and four geared toward affordable housing near mass transit — each with different rules.
 To win state dollars, developers often first have to secure funding from locally run affordable housing programs and bond measures — such as  the Proposition HHH homeless housing bond in Los Angeles — that have their own regulations.
 For developers, the process of cobbling together multiple streams of revenue is expensive. The Times found that each additional funding source increases the average per-unit cost by more than $6,000.
 The volume of financing requirements leads developers to hire consultants, attorneys and compliance officers. The U.S. GAO study found that 14% of the price tag for California’s affordable housing projects was made up of these types of administrative costs — the highest in the country and more than developers spend on land.
 As a result, there’s a huge backlog of projects, said Matt Schwartz, president of the California Housing Partnership Corp., which helps developers secure financing.
 “The folks in our office who work on all these applications, they’re just going bananas,” Schwartz said. “We can’t keep up with the workload.”
 The state is so overwhelmed that at a public meeting last summer, a top deputy with the Treasurer’s Office admitted that he had recently discovered piles of paper stacked in an office because staff couldn’t process them quickly enough.
 Newsom has promised to lead an effort this year to simplify how developers get state money for affordable housing.
  “I’ve just had enough with TCAC and CDLAC and OPRs and CalVets and HCDs and CalHFAs,” the governor said, name-dropping the alphabet soup of departments involved in financing projects, while unveiling his budget proposal in January. “Six of you understand what the hell I just said. No one else does. And that’s the point.”
   The Solana Beach City Council finally approved the Pearl at the end of a four-hour meeting in spring 2014.
  Almost immediately, the association for the condominium complex across the street sued, arguing that the city couldn’t build housing on the land because the deed required it to be a parking lot. The condo association lost the case and an appeal, but it took 2½ years to resolve.
  Afterward, Hitzke was able to win some tax credits from the state, but the legal delay presented her with a new challenge: construction costs.
 She attributes nearly all the 62% increase in the price per apartment in the six years since the project was approved to labor and materials. Overall, the UC Berkeley study found that those costs have gone up 30% for California affordable housing projects in that time.
 With a cost of building at $1.1 million per apartment, Hitzke hasn’t been able to find enough state and local money to break ground. The state and local governments have refused to give her the $1 million she still needed. And neighbors have never stopped fighting the development.
 Ernest Kurschat, a resident of a timeshare next to the site proposed for the Pearl, wrote to oppose the project in 2018, speculating that the retail space in the building would be “a food stamp office for the low-income housing.”
 Solana Beach leaders have continued to support the Pearl, however, contending much of the costs of building are unavoidable in a community where the median home value is $1.4 million.
 Hitzke said she’d have enough money to build the development, which is less than a mile from a major commuter rail stop, if she didn’t have to include the large underground parking garage.
  But City Councilman David Zito defended the garage. Even if Solana Beach were legally allowed to do away with the parking, he said, the city was “ethically, morally obligated” to replace it because of “residential harmony.” It’s the same reason he believed the Pearl’s height should fit in with the surrounding neighborhood — so it doesn’t remind people of notorious high-rise public housing.
 “You start talking about big projects that are wholly affordable and people start thinking about ‘projects,’ right? Like Cabrini-Green in Chicago or something like that,” said Zito, the only elected official who voted to approve the Pearl who remains on the council. “That then turns into a whole different discussion.”
  Last year, the Bay Area city of Alameda opened Everett Commons, a low-income development providing homes for 20 families at a cost of $947,000 per apartment to build. That project did not face the same level of opposition as the Pearl. But in the decade before Everett Commons’ approval, the number of proposed apartments shrank from 36 to 20.
  John Knox White, who voted for Everett Commons while on the planning commission and now serves on the City Council, said he regrets how little the city prioritized low-income families who were being forced out of Alameda.
 It’s a system that “provides excessive and undue voice and influence for people who are connected to the political process,” he said. “Who is speaking and who is being heard is very skewed, and it’s skewed toward people who have land and property.”
  Even if state officials are able to streamline bureaucracy and local officials are able to fend off naysaying neighbors, other drivers of California’s high housing costs remain tied to goals embraced by key Democratic constituencies: labor unions and environmentalists.
 Many state and local funding programs require low-income housing developers to pay union-level wages to construction workers. In Los Angeles, for example, these rules require developers to pay plumbers $51 an hour compared with their $24-an-hour median wage in the region.
 The UC Berkeley report found that projects paying union-level wages can cost $50,000 more per apartment. A Times analysis found a similar trend.
  California labor leaders cite other research,   including their own, concluding that the cost impacts are much lower. Beyond that, they contend that construction workers should earn enough money when building low-income housing so that they won’t need low-income housing themselves. State lawmakers   often repeat that argument.
 To have the best chance of winning tax credit funding, low-income housing developers also must build their projects to environmental standards that exceed even what the state requires of developers of new luxury condominiums. That includes using solar power for most of their electricity or certifying their energy efficiency with LEED or other third parties.
 The thinking is state-funded affordable housing should be “the most superior, energy-efficient buildings in the world,” said Doug Shoemaker, a senior vice president at nonprofit developer Mercy Housing. “I’m not sure that that last bit of benefit really is worth the cost.”
  The UC Berkeley study found that projects built to stricter environmental standards cost $17,000 more per apartment than those that aren’t.
 The Pearl’s funding requirements called for the building to be LEED-certified and for Hitzke to pay union-level wages for construction workers. The initial budget had left room for a profit. But as early as 2013, Hitzke realized that she wasn’t going to make any money on the deal.
  In December, the state informed her that it was planning to pull her funding because the project hadn’t broken ground. A month ago, Hitzke met with city officials to tell them she wanted out of her development agreement.
 “I am thrilled to be done with this thing,” Hitzke said. “It’s done nothing but kill me. But at the same time, I tried to do everything I could to get it done.”
 Zamora and his family, meanwhile, are still waiting in their apartment five miles away. When he drives by his old community these days, he feels only disappointment.

也许你很伤心，垂头丧气，步履蹒跚。也许你是愤怒和警觉，直挺挺地匆匆前行。或者，也许你在街上蹦蹦跳跳，快乐得令人恼火。无论你的步态是什么，再加上你的面部表情，这都是在非语言上向其他人发出信号，他们应该给你多少空间--能够准确地阅读这些暗示是我们社会物种的一项基本技能。
也许对机器人来说也是如此。马里兰大学的研究人员开发了一种名为ProxEmo的算法，这种算法使小型轮式机器人能够实时分析你的步态，并猜测你的感觉。根据感知到的情感，机器人可以选择自己的路线，给你或多或少的空间。对于机器人与人类的互动来说，这似乎是一件小事，但我们也可能会想象，有一天，当机器足够复杂时，它们可能会读懂一个悲伤的人的步态，并试图帮助他们。
“如果有人感到悲伤或困惑，机器人可以走到那个人面前说，哦，你今天看起来很难过，需要帮助吗？”帮助开发ProxEmo的马里兰大学机器人和人工智能研究员阿尼基特·贝拉(Aniket Bera)说。或者，解释步态的能力可能会帮助机器人在不想与其互动的人周围导航。“就像如果一个愤怒的人走向它，它会给你更多的空间，而不是一个悲伤的人，甚至是一个快乐的人，”他说。
直截了当地提醒一下：这个情感智能的机器人是基于感知到的情感来行动的。它不能真正读懂任何人的内心状态。即使是人类也不能百分之百肯定地看着另一个人，说他们知道那个人是高兴的、悲伤的还是生气的。但是，作为一个社会物种，我们已经学会了从一个人的步态中读出表明他们情绪状态的蛛丝马迹。这对不惹恼已经被激怒的人类同胞很有用。
因此，为了建立这个系统，机器人研究人员从人类开始。他们让一群人看着其他人走路，并问了他们一系列关于他们认为步行者的情绪状态是什么的问题。当然，这个练习在很大程度上是主观的。但研究人员从观察同伴的人那里获取了这些数据，然后将其与每个人的步态数据相关联。
然而，为了理解步态的样子，机器人需要客观的数据，而不是主观的判断。因此，研究人员使用算法分析了人们行走的视频，每个人的图像都覆盖了一个有16个关节的骨骼，包括颈部、肩膀和膝盖。然后，他们使用深度学习算法让系统将某些骨骼步态与人类志愿者与那些行走的人联系在一起的情绪联系起来。
他们最终采用了新的ProxEmo算法。他们把它装进一个可爱的黄色四轮机器人(来自机器人公司Clearpath的Jackal)，上面安装了一个摄像头。当机器人滚动时，摄像头会观察路过的行人，而ProxEmo会在每个人身上覆盖16个关节的骨骼-这是对步态的客观测量，算法已经学会了将其与某些情绪联系起来。这就是ProxEmo猜测你的情绪状态，并引导机器人尊重你的个人泡沫的方式。如果系统认为你生气了，这个泡沫可能会变大，如果它认为你很高兴，这个泡沫可能会缩小。
但是这个气泡并不是一个完美的圆形球体--它是长方形的，一种椭球体。贝拉说：“所以你前面有更多的空间，两边有一些空间，而你后面的个人空间更少。”当机器人迎面接近人类时，它需要在前面给出很大的空间，但当它从侧面经过或一旦它在人类后面恢复原来的路线时，就不需要这么做了。
然而，棘手的是，机器人是根据从实验室环境中行走的人提取的干净数据进行训练的-也就是说，他们的四肢在任何时候都是可见的。但在现实世界中，情况并不总是如此。因此，为了让机器人为一个混乱的世界做好准备，研究人员不得不以“噪音”的形式融入一点混沌。在这种情况下，它不是指声音，而是训练它处理各种变量，比如衣服和人是否携带可能遮住手的东西。贝拉说：“增加噪音不会改变人们的情绪，但我们已经添加了不同种类的噪音，然后训练系统即使一只手不见了，你也会盯着那个人看一段时间。”
有朝一日，ProxEmo可能会与另一个能读懂面部暗示的系统配对，建立一个更复杂的情商，这使得它更有可能知道它是应该请求帮助，还是应该对这个人敬而远之。如果我们想让机器人在不伤害人或自己的情况下在我们中间行走和滚动，那么它们必须在识别这些细微差别方面做得更好。
麻省理工学院计算机科学家迪娜·卡塔比(Dina Katabi)没有参与这项研究，她说：“当机器人以不自然的方式行事时，不仅会让人感到不舒服，还可能导致碰撞，因为人无法猜测这个机器人是如何移动的。”“因此，举个例子，如果一个机器人可以避免因为它离得太近而让人感到不舒服，那将是有益的。”
毕竟，把一个花哨的机器人变成一个非常昂贵的出气筒是没有意义的。

Maybe you’re sad, plodding along with your head slumped and shoulders sagging. Maybe you’re angry and alert, hurrying along upright. Or maybe you’re annoyingly happy, skipping down the street. Whatever your gait, that combined with your facial expression is nonverbally signaling to other people how much space they should give you—and being able to accurately read these cues is an essential skill for our social species.
 And perhaps for robots, too. Researchers at the University of Maryland have developed an algorithm called  ProxEmo, which gives a little wheeled robot the power to analyze your gait in real time, and to take a guess at how you might be feeling. Based on that perceived emotion, the robot can choose its route to give you more or less space. This might seem like a small matter for robot-human interactions, but we might also imagine that one day, when machines are sophisticated enough, they might read a sad person’s gait and try to help them.
 “If somebody's feeling sad or confused, the robot can go up to the person and say,  Oh you're looking sad today, do you need help?” says Aniket Bera, a robotics and AI researcher at the University of Maryland, who helped develop ProxEmo. Or, the ability to interpret gait might help the robot navigate around a person who wouldn’t want to interact with it. “Like if an angry person were to walk towards it, it would give more space, as opposed to a sad person or even a happy person,” he says.
  A caveat right off the bat: This emotionally intelligent robot is basing its actions on  perceived emotions. It can’t truly read anyone’s inner state. Not even a human can look at another and say with 100 percent confidence that they know if that person is happy, sad, or angry. But as a social species, we’ve learned to read telltale clues in a person’s gait that indicate their emotional state. That’s useful for not pissing off an already pissed-off fellow human.
  So to build this system, the robotics researchers began with humans. They had a group of people look at other people walking, and asked them a series of questions about what they thought the walker’s emotional state was. This exercise was largely subjective, of course. But the researchers took this data from people observing their fellow humans and then correlated it with data about each person’s gait.
  In order to understand how a gait looks, though, the robot needs objective data, not subjective judgements. So the researchers used algorithms that analyzed videos of the people walking, with each person’s image overlaid by a skeleton with 16 joints, including at the neck, shoulders, and knees. Then, they used deep learning algorithms to get the system to associate certain skeletal gaits with the emotions that the human volunteers associated with those walking people.
  They ended up with the new ProxEmo algorithm. They load this into a cute little yellow four-wheeled robot (the Jackal, from the robotics company Clearpath) with a camera mounted on it. As the robot rolls along, the camera watches passing pedestrians, while ProxEmo overlays each human with that 16-joint skeleton—the objective measurement of gaits, which the algorithm has learned to associate with certain emotions. That’s how ProxEmo can take a guess at your emotional state, and direct the robot to respect your personal bubble, which may grow larger if the system thinks you’re angry, and shrink if it thinks you’re happy.
  But this bubble isn’t a perfectly rounded sphere—it’s oblong, a kind of ellipsoid. “So there's more space ahead of you, there's some space at the sides, and there's less personal space behind you,” says Bera. When the robot is approaching a human head-on, it needs to give a lot of space up front, but less so as it passes on the side or when it resumes its original course once it’s behind the human.
  The tricky bit, though, is that the robot trained on clean data extracted from people walking in a lab environment—that is, their limbs were all visible at all times. But that isn’t always the case in the real world. So to prepare the robot for a chaotic world, the researchers had to incorporate a little bit of chaos in the form of “noise.” In this context, it doesn’t mean sound, but training it to handle variables like clothing and if the person is carrying something that might cover their hand. “Adding noise won't change the emotion, but we've added different kinds of noises, and then trained the system to understand even if one hand is missing, you've still been looking at that person for a while,” says Bera.
 One day, ProxEmo might be paired with another system that reads facial cues, building an even more complex emotional intelligence, which makes it more likely to know if it should ask to be of assistance, or give the person a wide berth. Robots have to get much, much better at picking up such nuances, if we want them to walk and roll among us without hurting people or themselves.
 “When robots behave in an unnatural way, not only can people become uncomfortable, but also it can lead potentially to collisions, because the person cannot guess how this robot is moving,” says MIT computer scientist Dina Katabi, who wasn’t involved in this work. “So if a robot, for example, can avoid making people uncomfortable because it's getting too close, that would be beneficial.”
 No sense, after all, in turning a fancy robot into a very expensive punching bag.

随着冠状病毒导致的经济余波进一步蔓延，数百万美国人正在努力偿还债务。
信用报告机构TransUnion的最新报告显示，4月份进入财务困境状态的信用卡账户比例飙升至3.2%，即1,470万个账户。这一比例在4月份飙升，而2020年3月仅为0.01%，去年同期为0.03%。
汽车贷款也陷入了类似的困境，上个月有3.5%，即近300万个账户无法支付月度还款。
TransUnion表示，目前约有270万笔抵押贷款，即此类贷款的5%处于财务困境。住房贷款违约率较一年前有所下降，尽管使用忍耐计划可能会推迟违约率的上升。
包括忍耐和延期计划在内的救济计划允许借款人推迟一些付款，并优先偿还哪些债务。TransUnion负责研究和咨询的副总裁马特·科莫斯(Matt Komos)说，政府的2万亿美元刺激法案为那些苦苦挣扎于信贷产品的人提供了一些选择，但国会可能需要采取进一步行动。
他在周三的报告中表示，虽然这些计划为消费者提供了暂时的缓解，但银行和贷款人正在寻找进一步的监管指导，以确定一旦刺激计划枯竭，下一步应该采取什么措施。
虽然冠状病毒大流行最初是作为全球生产商的供应链问题出现的，但它很快就成为近一个世纪以来最大的经济威胁。飙升的失业率使得更多的美国人每月支付利息的能力下降，而长达数年的借款增加趋势势必加剧这个问题。
根据TransUnion的数据，第一季度每个借款人的平均信用卡债务达到5,653美元，比去年同期增加了近100美元。拖欠率和信用卡总数也比2019年第一季度大幅上升。
借款人没有帮助就无法生存。在美国疫情爆发后，几家贷款机构为贷款和信用卡提供了免费忍耐选项。然而，短期解决方案给贷款人带来了压力，并有可能在最后期限恢复后引发一波违约浪潮。
note_en:
Millions of Americans are struggling to pay off debt as coronavirus-led economic fallout spreads further.
 The share of credit-card accounts entering "financial hardship" status spiked to 3.2%, or 14.7 million accounts, in April, according to a new  report from credit-reporting agency TransUnion. The proportion spiked higher in April from just 0.01% in March 2020 and 0.03% in the year-ago period.
 Auto loans posted a similar slide into hardship, with 3.5%, or nearly three million accounts, unable to cover monthly payments last month.
 About 2.7 million mortgages, or 5% of such loans, are currently in financial hardship, TransUnion said. Home loan delinquencies have dropped from their year-ago readings, though usage of forbearance programs could delay an uptick in defaults.
   Relief programs including forbearance and deferment plans allow borrowers to delay some payments and prioritize which debts to pay off first. The government's $2 trillion stimulus bill opened some options for those struggling with credit products, but Congress will likely need to act further, Matt Komos, vice president of research and consulting at TransUnion, said.
  "While these programs are providing consumers with temporary relief, banks and lenders are looking for further regulatory guidance as to what next steps should be taken once stimulus packages dry up," he said in the Wednesday report.
 While the coronavirus pandemic first emerged as a supply-chain issue for global producers, it's quickly emerged as the biggest economic threat in nearly a century. Soaring unemployment left more Americans less able to make monthly interest payments, and a years-long trend in increased borrowing stands to exacerbate the problem.
   The average credit card debt per borrower reached $5,653 in the first quarter, up nearly $100 from the year-ago period, according to TransUnion. Delinquency rates and the gross number of credit cards have also jumped from the first quarter of 2019.
 Borrowers aren't without help. Several lenders have offered free forbearance options for loans and credit cards in the wake of the US outbreak. Yet the short-term solution places pressure on lenders and risks a wave of defaults once deadlines are reinstated.

note:
跳到1月份的主要内容，随着一种新型冠状病毒从中国传出，马克·扎克伯格(Mark Zuckerberg)开始为潜在的大流行做准备。他开始将他的团队转移到一些项目上，这些项目将在随后在世界各地的长期在家订单期间发挥作用，同时也会强调Facebook巨大规模和覆盖范围的一些更积极的方面。
在几周内，Facebook就将一个现有的与疾病研究人员合作的计划变成了一张实时地图，显示了在家工作订单的有效性。它与卡内基梅隆大学(Carnegie Mellon University)合作，就潜在的冠状病毒症状对用户进行调查，努力为公共卫生研究人员确定新的热点。
在视频会议公司Zoom从每天1000万会议参与者激增到3亿之后，Facebook推出了一个名为Room的竞争对手。本周早些时候，它宣布了商店，这是进军电子商务的重大新举措。
然而，与扎克伯格周四上午在对员工的直播中宣布的消息相比，所有这些都相形见绌。从今天开始，该公司将其在美国的大部分公开职位提供给远程招聘和招聘。今年晚些时候，Facebook在世界各地的48,000名员工中的许多人将能够请求切换到远程工作。扎克伯格预测，在未来十年内，Facebook-一家直到最近还向住在门洛帕克总部附近的新员工支付高达1.5万美元奖金的公司-可能会在很大程度上成为远程员工。
“我认为我们可以让公司大约一半的员工长期远程工作。”
扎克伯格说：“在我们这个规模的远程工作领域，我们将成为最具前瞻性的公司。”“我们需要以一种深思熟虑和负责任的方式来做这件事，所以我们将以一种有节制的方式来做这件事。但我认为，在接下来的5到10年里--可能更接近10年，而不是5年，但在这个范围内的某个地方--我认为我们可能会让公司大约一半的员工长期远程工作。“。
这一决定标志着这家世界上最重要的公司之一的文化发生了重大转变-但不一定会带来巨大的成本节约。扎克伯格表示，新的开支，包括让员工偶尔来总部参加“OnSites”--相当于COVID后的异地务虚会--可能会弥补Facebook在房地产和员工工资方面节省的任何成本。
在宣布这一消息的前夕，我们与扎克伯格讨论了他为什么改变了对远程工作的看法，仍未发明的在家工作工具，以及他自己在后COVID时代在办公室工作的计划。
马克·扎克伯格：从明天开始，我们下一步要做的是开启远程招聘。这在某种程度上是有道理的，因为现在，每个人几乎都在远程工作，但我们仍然将招聘限制在居住在不开放办公室周围的人身上。所以我们要开始远程招聘了。
然后在现有员工方面，我们将允许人们在某个时候申请成为永久远程员工。显然，他们不需要现在就做出这个决定。我们已经宣布，如果人们愿意，他们可以远程工作到2020年底。如果COVID仍然盛行，有可能会延伸到更远的地方。但从长远来看，我们将允许人们请求永久远程工作。我们将把重点放在有经验的员工身上，而不是刚毕业的大学毕业生身上，我认为他们需要更多地呆在办公室里进行培训。
从历史上看，你付钱让人们住在离办公室近的地方，这表明这对你来说真的很重要。在过去的几个月里，你看到了什么导致你的想法发生了这种转变？
我想是几件事。其一，我们正在开发大量的远程呈现技术和产品。从我们正在研究的私人通信工具，到用于企业通信的Workplace，再到我们正在推出的一系列[新]功能的远程呈现门户，应有尽有。从长远来看，VR和AR都是为人们提供远程体验。因此，如果你长期使用VR、AR和视频聊天，你必须相信你正在帮助人们在任何地方做他们想做的任何事情。因此，我认为这表明了一种世界观，这将导致随着时间的推移，人们可以更远程地工作。
不过，最直接的驱动因素显然是COVID，它极大地加速了这一进程。我认为，在一段时间内远离家乡的经历比预期的要积极得多-并不是没有问题。但我也认为有一个实际的因素，那就是很多人在一段时间内不能回到办公室。即使考虑到社交距离，我们认为办公室的密度也将达到25%左右。所以，这只是意味着我们将会有很多人想要回到办公室，但他们做不到。
“我们会有很多人想回到办公室，但做不到。”
因此，考虑到人们将在一段时间内远程工作，我只是觉得我们必须在这方面做得很好。考虑到这么长的时间，我认为这是一个我们无论如何都会想要更多努力的方向，看起来我们应该继续前进。
但我们正在以一种有条不紊的方式来做这件事。有些人可能只想让我们说‘好吧，公司里的任何人现在都可以决定远程办公，想去哪里买房子就去哪里。’而这并不是真正的方法。如果你有经验，如果你在公司内部处于某个级别，如果你有良好的绩效评级，如果你所在的团队将支持远程工作，如果你获得了批准，那么你现在就可以知道你将成为一名永久的远程员工。然后，随着时间的推移，我们会随着时间的推移更多地开放它。但这件事太重要了，不能只说每个人都可以去做，然后在路上弄清楚。
你说Facebook可能会有多达一半的员工成为远程员工。你是怎么达到这个目标的？
我实际上不会说这是一个目标或目标-我认为这更多的是一个预测。这就是我是怎么到那里的。我们做了这些调查，询问人们他们想做什么。我们现有员工中有20%表示，他们对远程全职工作非常感兴趣。此外，另有20%的人表示，他们对此有一定的兴趣。所以我认为基本上会发生的是，因为需要一段时间才能让每个人回到办公室，你已经有大约40%的员工相当愿意远程工作。
也许这并不是所有40%的人都会选择呆在偏远的地方。此外，在这40%的人中，有些人将在没有资格远程工作的团队中。但是，你必须将未来五年我们将经历的所有远程招聘叠加在所有这些人的远程招聘中，这些人将生活在他们唯一选择远程工作的地方，或者搬到不同的地方。所以我可以想象，在5到10年的时间里，我们可能会再雇佣公司20%左右的员工，他们基本上都在他们唯一的选择是远程工作的地方。最重要的是，从长远来看，我认为你可以达到现有员工的20%到30%，他们既想远程工作，又有资格这样做。这样你就到了50岁左右。
本周，我们就长期远程工作问题采访了谷歌的桑达尔·皮查伊(Sundar Pichai)。他说，他仍在考虑他的团队完成建造他们在大流行开始之前就已经在研究的东西后会发生什么。在一个你并不总是在电梯里撞人的环境中，你如何集思广益，做创造性的工作？
这是最大的悬而未决的问题之一。让人们大吃一惊的是，人们在家工作的效率比人们预期的要高。有些人认为一切都会崩溃，但事实并非如此。很多人实际上都在说，他们现在的生产力更高了。
“有些人认为一切都会分崩离析，事实并非如此。”
但我认为，从长远来看，更大的问题是你在说什么。这是社会关系，是文化，是创造力。有很多工具都需要围绕这一点来构建。这就是为什么我没有说我希望每个人立即远程工作的部分原因。虽然我们的手有点受到COVID和社会距离的影响，但在短期内我们可能仍然会比我认为的理想情况更遥远。
我一直担心的一件事是，在家里工作似乎对那些事业发展相对较远的人来说是相当好的，他们可能不需要太多的指导、指导和网络。你说过你不太可能让应届大学毕业生远程工作。为什么会这样呢？
它的基本版本只是，他们以前从未在公司工作过，需要学习如何在公司工作。当我试图仔细考虑我们的政策时，我采访过的大多数偏远公司-我发现有趣的一件事是，他们倾向于不聘用应届大学毕业生。他们基本上会说，‘我们只会雇佣大学毕业几年的人。’我们战略的很大一部分是，我们每年雇佣数千名应届大学毕业生，我们将继续这样做。
但我确实认为，在这种情况下，这是一个不同的挑战，所以我们计划要求应届大学毕业生进入办公室接受培训-或者至少，这是一个长期的想法。在COVID期间移动，这将是更具挑战性的。我们有数以千计的实习生很快就会来到这家公司-他们将是偏远的，所以这将是一个有趣的实验，看看情况会如何。我们总是从实习生那里学到很多东西。
让数千名Facebook员工以更分散的方式工作，你看到了哪些好处？
其一是获得更广泛的人才库。所以现在，我们把自己限制在少数几个城市。这并不是一个太糟糕的限制，但更广泛的开放肯定是有好处的。所以我想那会很好。优势不仅在招聘方面--在留住方面也是如此。一群离开公司的人，他们是我们想要留住的好人-他们离开的原因是他们想要搬到我们不支持的地方。因此，远程工作将帮助我们留住这些关键人员，这在很多方面都比不得不招聘新人要好。因此，对双方来说，这都将帮助我们接触到更多的人才。
“我认为这将帮助我们推进我们正在研究的一些未来技术。”
另一件事是，我认为它将帮助我们推进我们在远程呈现方面正在研究的一些未来技术，因为我们自己也会不断地使用它。像视频聊天这样的东西我们已经用了一整天了。我们赖以生存的工作场所。但我认为，对于VR和AR来说，这可能有助于加快这一进程。目前，VR和AR在公司内是一个很大的群体，但它仍然与大多数员工的日常工作有些脱节。我认为这可以更快地改变这一点。所以这是我特别兴奋的事情。
就公司的利益而言，我应该提到的另一件事是多样性。我们只需要接触到生活在不同地方、来自不同社区、不同背景的人。因此，每一种衡量多样性的标准--背景和意识形态--我认为我们都能接触到更多的人。
对于世界来说，我认为更公平地分配机会。与其强迫人们到城市来寻找机会，你将能够更多地传播这种机会。那太好了。然后我认为这其中有一个很大的环境方面。人们不再通勤，他们也不会像往返那样频繁地飞来飞去。今天公布的一些统计数据显示，自COVID开始以来，排放量大幅下降了约1%。这种情况不会完全持续下去，但在2020年，移动比特要比原子容易得多。因此，我更愿意让我们通过虚拟现实或视频聊天进行心灵传送，而不是坐在车流中。
我想这是您自己远程工作过的最长的一段时间。你自己对在家工作的想法是如何演变的？
我绝对认为这是我远程工作时间最长的一次。它也比我想象的更有成效。但这也是一个非常反常的时期。这有点难以推论。我从在其他远程公司工作过的人那里听到的一件事是，虽然对许多人来说，这段时间比在办公室工作效率更高，但比起以前全职远程工作时的感觉，工作效率更低-因为你会像周围的孩子一样分心，每个人都对COVID感到超级紧张。所以这不是一个稳定的环境。这很有趣。
“我们现在在多大程度上只是从过去10年建立起来的文化和方向中走出来？”
但我认为其中一个很大的未知数是你之前谈到的，在创造力方面。我们现在在多大程度上只是在起草我们在过去10年中建立起来的文化和方向？未来可能很难改变现状。到目前为止，我的经验并不是改变事情有那么难-我们确实改变了一系列东西的方向，加快了一系列东西的开发，我们已经看到很多这样的东西在这一点上推出了。因此，就我领导公司实现这一目标的能力而言，我对此更为乐观。但随着时间的推移，我们会看到--有很多未知数。
假设在未来的某个时候会有冠状病毒疫苗。在这一点上，你个人认为自己在办公室工作与远程工作相比有多大程度上的差异？
这是个好问题。我不是..。正常，就我必须做的事情而言。我得去旅行才能去看人。商业伙伴、政府官员和不同的人走进办公室，即使我想要完全远程工作，对我来说也是不可能的。
但我认为，考虑到这种精神，希望与员工保持联系，出于一些相同的原因，希望使用我们正在开发的一些更先进的技术-我确实认为，随着时间的推移，我会计划花更多的时间远程访问。但我正在想像我这个角色的人会是什么样子。
note_en:
Skip to main content                                                                                                                In January, as reports about the spread of a novel coronavirus emerged from China, Mark Zuckerberg began to prepare for a potential pandemic. He began turning his teams to projects that would be useful during the long stay-at-home orders that would follow around the world — and would also highlight some of the more positive aspects of Facebook’s vast size and reach.
 Within weeks, Facebook had turned an existing program for working with disease researchers into  a live map showing the effectiveness of stay-at-home orders. It partnered with Carnegie Mellon University to  survey users on potential COVID-19 symptoms in an effort to identify new hotspots for public health researchers.
 After the videoconferencing company Zoom  surged from 10 million daily meeting participants to 300 million, Facebook  introduced a competitor named Rooms. Earlier this week,  it announced Shops, a significant new push into e-commerce.
 All of that pales, though, next to  the announcement Zuckerberg made Thursday morning in a live stream to his employees. Beginning today, the company is making most of its open roles in the United States available for remote recruiting and hiring. And later this year, many of Facebook’s 48,000 employees around the world will be able to request a switch to remote work. Within the next decade, Zuckerberg predicts, Facebook — a company that until recently paid new hires a bonus of up to $15,000 to live near its Menlo Park headquarters — could be a largely remote workforce.
 “I think we could get to about half of the company working remotely permanently.”
 “We’re going to be the most forward-leaning company on remote work at our scale,” Zuckerberg says. “We need to do this in a way that’s thoughtful and responsible, so we’re going to do this in a measured way. But I think that it’s possible that over the next five to 10 years — maybe closer to 10 than five, but somewhere in that range — I think we could get to about half of the company working remotely permanently.”
 The decision marks a monumental shift in the culture of one of the world’s most consequential companies — and not necessarily one that will result in big cost savings. Zuckerberg said that new expenses, including bringing employees to headquarters for occasional “onsites” — the post-COVID equivalent of offsite retreats — will likely make up for any money Facebook saves on reduced costs associated with real estate and employee salaries.
 On the eve of the announcement, we talked with Zuckerberg about why he changed his views on remote work, the work-from-home tools still yet to be invented, and his own plans for working from an office in a post-COVID future.
   Mark Zuckerberg: The next step of what we’re doing, starting tomorrow, is we’re unlocking remote hiring. It just kind of makes sense because, right now, everyone is pretty much working remotely, but we’re still just constraining our hiring to people who live around an office which isn’t open. So we’re going to start remote hiring.
 Then on the existing employees, we’re going to allow people to request to be a permanent remote worker at some point. And they don’t have to make that decision right now, obviously. We already announced that people can remote work through the end of 2020 if they want. And if COVID is still prevalent, it’s possible that that extends beyond that. But on a long-term basis, we’re going to let people request to work permanently remotely. We’re going to focus on experienced employees rather than new college grads, who I think need to be in the office more, for training.
 Historically, you’ve paid people to live close to the office, suggesting that that was really important to you. What did you see over the past couple of months that led to this shift in your thinking?
 I think it’s a few things. One is that we’re working on a lot of remote presence technology and products. Everything from the private communication stuff that we’re working on, to Workplace for enterprise communication, to Portal for remote presence, which we’re rolling out a bunch of [new] features around. And then on the long-term stuff, VR and AR is all about giving people remote presence. So if you’re long on VR and AR and video chat, you have to believe in some capacity that you’re helping people be able to do whatever they want from wherever they are. So I think that that suggests a worldview that would lead to allowing people to work more remotely over time.
 The immediate driver, though, that’s accelerated this dramatically is COVID, obviously. And I think that the experience of being remote for some period has been more positive than expected — not without issues. But I also just think that there’s this practical element, which is that a lot of people aren’t going to be able to go back to the offices for a while. Even with social distancing, we think that the offices are going to be at about 25 percent density. So, that just means that we’re going to have a lot of people who want to go back to the offices but aren’t going to be able to.
 “We’re going to have a lot of people who want to go back to the offices but aren’t going to be able to.”
 So, given that people are going to be remote working for a while, I just kind of feel like we have to get good at it. And given that long term, this is a direction that I think we’re going to want to go in more anyway, it just seemed like we should move forward.
 But we’re doing it in a methodical way. Some people would want us to just say ‘Okay, anyone in the company can can just decide now that they want to work remotely, and go buy a house wherever they want.’ And that’s not really the approach. If you’re experienced, if you’re at a certain level within the company, if you have good performance ratings, if you’re on a team that’s going to support remote work, and if you get approval, then you’ll be able to know now that you’ll be a permanent remote worker. And then we’ll open it up more over time as we learn. But this is too important of a thing to just say everyone can go do this, and then kind of figure it out along the way.
 You say up to half of Facebook could become remote workers. How did you arrive at that goal?
 I wouldn’t actually say that it’s a target or goal — I think it’s more of a prediction. Here’s how I got there. We ran these surveys and asked people what they want to do. Twenty percent of our existing employees said that they were extremely or very interested in working remotely full time. And another 20 percent on top of that said that they were somewhat interested. So I think what’s basically going to happen is that, because it’s going to take a while to get everyone back into the office, you have like 40 percent of employees already who were fairly willing to work remotely.
 Maybe it’s not all 40 percent will choose to stay remote. And also, of those 40 percent, some of those will be on teams that are not eligible to work remote. But then you have to layer in all the remote recruiting that we’re going to go through over the next five years of all these people who are going to live in places where their only choice is to work remote, or move to a different location. So I would imagine that over five to 10 years, we’ll probably have hired another 20 percent of the company or so who basically are in places where their only choice is really to work remotely. And then on top of that, over the long term, I think you can get to 20 to 30 percent of existing employees who both want to work remotely and are eligible to do so. And so you get to around 50.
 This week   we interviewed Sundar Pichai at Google  about long-term remote work. He said he was still thinking about what happens after his team gets through building the things they were already working on before the pandemic started. How do you brainstorm and do creative work in an environment where you’re not always bumping into people in the elevators?
 That’s one of the big open questions. The thing that’s been positively surprising to people is that people are more productive working at home than people would have expected. Some people thought that everything was just going to fall apart, and it hasn’t. And a lot of people are actually saying that they’re more productive now.
 “Some people thought that everything was just going to fall apart, and it hasn’t.”
 But I think the bigger question, longer term, is what you’re saying. It’s the social connections, it’s the culture, and it’s creativity. And there are a lot of tools that just need to get built around that. That’s part of the reason why I’m not saying I want everyone to go work remotely immediately. Although our hand is forced a little bit there by COVID and social distancing, so we’ll probably still be more remote in the near term than I think would be ideal.
 One of the things that I’ve been worried about as I’ve thought through this is that it seems like working from home is probably pretty good for people who are relatively far along in their careers, who might not need as much coaching, mentoring, and networking. You said that you’re less likely to let new college grads work remotely. Why is that?
 The basic version of it is just, they’ve never worked at a company before and need to learn how to work at a company. Most remote companies that I’ve talked to when trying to think through our policies — one of the things that I found interesting is that they tend to not hire new college grads. They basically say, ‘We’re only going to hire people who are a couple years out of college.’ And a huge part of our strategy has been, we hire thousands of new college grads a year, and we’re going to continue doing that.
 But I do think that’s just a different challenge in this [situation], and so we are planning on requiring new college grads to come into the office for training — or at least, that’s the long-term idea. Moving during COVID, that will be more challenging. And we have thousands of interns who are descending on the company soon — they will be remote, so that will be an interesting experiment to see how that goes. We always learn a lot from interns.
 What are some of the benefits you see in having thousands of Facebook employees working in a more distributed way?
 One is access to a wider talent pool. So right now, we’re constraining ourselves to a small number of cities. It hasn’t been  too bad of a constraint, but certainly there’s an advantage to opening up more widely. So I think that’ll be good. The advantage is not just on the recruiting side — it’s also on the retention side. A bunch of the people who leave the company, who are good people who we would want to keep — the reason that they leave is because they want to move somewhere that we don’t support. So remote work will help us retain those key folks, which in a lot of ways is better than having to recruit a new person. So on both sides, it will help us access more talent.
 “I think it will help us advance some of the future technology we’re working on.”
 The other thing is that I think it will help us advance some of the future technology we’re working on around remote presence, because we’re just going to be using it constantly ourselves. Things like video chat we already use all day long. Workplace we live on. But I think for VR and AR, this could help accelerate those. Right now, VR and AR is a large group within the company, but it’s still somewhat disconnected from the work that most employees are doing on a day-to-day basis. And I think that this could change that sooner. So that’s something that I’m particularly excited about.
 Another thing that I should mention, in terms of benefit for the company, is diversity. We’ll just get access to people in different communities, from different backgrounds, who live in different places. So every measure of diversity — backgrounds and ideology — I think we’ll just have access to more folks.
 For the world, I think spreading opportunity more equally. Rather than forcing people to come to cities for opportunity, you’ll be able to spread that out more. That will be good. And then I think there’s a big environmental aspect of it. People aren’t commuting, and they’re not flying around as much. There’s some stat that came out today about how emissions are down some massive percent since COVID began. And that won’t exactly continue, but in 2020 it is a lot easier to move bits around than atoms. So I’d much rather have us teleport by using virtual reality or video chat than sit in traffic.
 I imagine that this is the longest stretch that you yourself have worked remotely. How have your own thoughts about working from home evolved?
 I definitely think this is the longest stretch that I have worked remotely. It’s also been more productive than I thought it was going to be. But it’s also such an anomalous period. It’s a little hard to extrapolate from. One of the things that I’ve heard from people who have worked at other remote companies is that this period, while it is more productive for a lot of people than being in the office, is less productive than they felt like they were when they worked full time remotely before — because you have distractions like kids around, and everyone is super stressed out about COVID. So it’s not a stable environment. That’s been interesting.
 “To what extent are we all now just drafting off of the culture and direction that we built up over the last 10 years?”
 But I think one of the big unknowns is what you were talking about earlier, in terms of creativity. To what extent are we all now just drafting off of the culture and direction that we built up over the last 10 years? It might just be very hard to change things going forward. Now, my experience so far has not been that it’s been that hard to change things — we’ve certainly changed direction on a bunch of stuff and accelerated development of a bunch of stuff, and we’ve seen a bunch of that stuff launched at this point. So I’m a little more optimistic about that, in terms of my ability to lead the company on that. But we’ll see over time — there’s a lot of unknowns.
 Say there’s a COVID-19 vaccine at some point in the future. At that point, how much do you personally see yourself working in an office versus working remotely?
 It’s a good question. I’m not... normal, in terms of the constraints of what I have to do. I have to travel to go see people. Business partners and government officials and different folks come into the office, and it just would not be possible, even if I wanted to, for me to work fully remotely.
 But I think given the spirit of this, and wanting to be in touch with employees, and for some of the same reasons around wanting to use some of our more advanced technology that we’re developing — I do think I’ll plan to spend more of my time remotely over time. But I’m figuring out exactly what that would look like for someone in my role.

文明六号提供了与你的世界互动的新方式，在地图上扩大你的帝国，推进你的文化，并与历史上最伟大的领导人竞争，建设一个经得起时间考验的文明。
看着你的帝国的奇迹以前所未有的方式在地图上铺展开来。每个城市跨越多个瓷砖，因此您可以自定义构建您的城市，以充分利用当地地形。
解锁可以加速你的文明在历史上的进步。为了更快地进步，请使用您的单位积极探索、开发您的环境，并发现新的文化。
与其他文明的互动在游戏过程中会发生变化，从最初的原始互动(冲突是生活的现实)到后期的联盟和谈判。
在“一砖一瓦”设计的基础上扩展，支援单位现在可以嵌入其他单位，比如步兵的反坦克支援，或者定居者的战士支援。类似的部队也可以合并成强大的“兵团”部队。
除了传统的多人游戏模式之外，你还可以在各种各样的情况下与你的朋友合作和竞争，所有这些都是为了在一次会话中轻松完成。
文明六号为老玩家提供了新的方法来构建和调整他们的文明，以获得最大的成功机会。新的教程系统向新玩家介绍基本概念，因此他们可以轻松入门。
note_en:
Civilization VI offers new ways to interact with your world, expand your empire across the map, advance your culture, and compete against history’s greatest leaders to build a civilization that will stand the test of time.
  See the marvels of your empire spread across the map like never before. Each city spans multiple tiles so you can custom build your cities to take full advantage of the local terrain.
  Unlock boosts that speed your civilization’s progress through history. To advance more quickly, use your units to actively explore, develop your environment, and discover new cultures.
  Interactions with other civilizations change over the course of the game, from primitive first interactions where conflict is a fact of life, to late game alliances and negotiations.
  Expanding on the “one unit per tile” design, support units can now be embedded with other units, like anti-tank support with infantry, or a warrior with settlers. Similar units can also be combined to form powerful “Corps” units.
  In addition to traditional multiplayer modes, cooperate and compete with your friends in a wide variety of situations all designed to be easily completed in a single session.
  Civilization VI provides veteran players new ways to build and tune their civilization for the greatest chance of success. New tutorial systems introduce new players to the underlying concepts so they can easily get started.

note:
跳到主要内容，Facebook今天表示，它将开始允许大多数员工请求永久更换工作，以便让他们远程工作。此举表明，冠状病毒大流行正在以多快的速度重塑全球经济。该公司将从今天开始，使其在美国的大部分职位空缺都有资格进行远程招聘，并在今年晚些时候开始接受员工永久远程工作的申请。
首席执行官马克·扎克伯格在接受The Verge采访时表示：“我们将成为规模最大的远程办公领域最具前瞻性的公司。”“我们需要以一种深思熟虑和负责任的方式来做这件事，所以我们将以一种有节制的方式来做这件事。但我认为，在接下来的5到10年里--可能更接近10年，而不是5年，但在这个范围内的某个地方--我认为我们可能会让公司大约一半的员工长期远程工作。“。
Facebook在全球70个办事处拥有超过4.8万名员工，是疫情爆发后迄今最大的一家积极进军远程工作的公司。Twitter上周宣布，将为大多数员工提供远程工作的选择，Coinbase在周三也发布了类似的声明。Shopify首席执行官托比·吕克(Tobi Lütke)周四表示，将立即开始转向永久远程工作。谷歌首席执行官桑达尔·皮查伊(Sundar Pichai)本周告诉The Verge，公司正在考虑增加远程工作的灵活性，而不是让大多数员工呆在家里一直呆到年底。
总体而言，对远程工作的拥抱颠覆了硅谷数十年来的传统智慧，在硅谷，最大的公司都是建立在物理上紧密协作的理念之上的。直到最近，如果新员工同意住在离办公室10英里的范围内，Facebook还会向他们支付高达15,000美元的奖金。现在，他们中的许多人将能够在他们喜欢的任何地方工作-尽管Facebook将降低搬到成本较低地区的员工的工资。
短期内，Facebook向远程工作的转变已被证明是必要的。该公司本周表示，当该公司7月6日开始重新开放部分办公室时，计划将入住率降至正常水平的25%。而且，进入办公室的额外安全要求，包括强制性口罩和体温检查，可能会让许多员工远离办公室更长时间。
但在对员工进行了调查，并与其他以远程员工为基础的公司的高管交谈后，扎克伯格表示，他开始相信更分散的劳动力的好处。他说，此举将向更广泛的申请者开放Facebook的工作岗位，并可能对环境产生积极影响。(其中一个原因是，远程工作可能会让数千名Facebook员工放弃每天长达数小时的往返门洛帕克总部的通勤。)。
此举还迫使Facebook依赖这些工具来完成工作。Facebook正在投资数十亿美元创建下一代通信工具。从这个意义上说，转向远程工作代表着一项高风险的举措，目的是让Facebook员工“狗粮”新产品，包括增强现实、虚拟现实、公司的门户智能显示器和Workplace协作工具-并出于需要更快地改进这些产品，因为公司依赖他们完成工作。
“你必须相信，你正在帮助人们在任何地方做他们想做的事情。”
扎克伯格说：“VR和AR都是为了给人们提供远程体验。”因此，如果你长期使用VR、AR和视频聊天，你必须相信你正在帮助人们在任何地方做他们想做的任何事情。因此，我认为这表明了一种世界观，随着时间的推移，这将使人们能够更远程地工作。“。
但此举也伴随着风险。自3月份以来，Facebook和其他大型科技公司的员工一直在制定计划，这些计划是由校园里的员工共同发起的。在没有围绕同一张会议桌进行头脑风暴的环境中，公司能否在长期内保持同样的创造力或生产力仍然是一个悬而未决的问题。(有人会说，Facebook近年来并不是特别有创意。)。
扎克伯格说，他预计将缓慢转向远程工作，但将首先向远程员工提供更多公司的开放角色。扎克伯格说，其他人可能会继续喜欢在公司办公室工作-特别是年轻员工。
扎克伯格说，另一个维护团队同志情谊的努力将来自定期邀请员工进入校园参加“OnSites”-这是校园内版本的场外务虚会，在大流行来袭之前，这种场外务虚会在商界很常见。他说，事实上，与现场会议相关的差旅成本可能会抵消公司原本预计的房地产足迹减少带来的许多节省。
“我们今天拥有的工具是相当交易性的--这是生态系统中的一个弱点。”
尽管如此，关于该公司转向远程工作的许多问题仍然存在。Facebook过去常常以弗兰克·盖里(Frank Gehry)设计的园区和奢侈的福利来吸引新员工；它将为在家工作的员工提供什么？大部分远程劳动力将如何影响围绕多样性和包容性的努力？在一个很少与导师面对面互动的世界里，年轻员工将如何找到导师呢？
“我认为我们今天拥有的工具是相当交易性的--这是生态系统中的一个弱点，”他说。“电子邮件和信息--这些东西是用来交流思想的，而不是用来闲逛的。这几乎就像是你真正想要的是拥有一个团队，只是在一起度过无节制的时间。“。
扎克伯格说，扎克伯格已经从每月邀请高层管理人员到他家共进晚餐，转变为使用公司的新客房产品。他还在尝试在虚拟现实中召开管理会议。
假设冠状病毒风险最终消退，扎克伯格计划至少部分回到办公室。从历史上看，CEO的角色需要出差和面对面的会议，而这些可能无法在网上完成。但扎克伯格表示，与大流行之前相比，他也可能会继续在家里工作更多时间。
“我确实认为，随着时间的推移，我会计划把更多的时间花在远程上，”他说。
阅读The Verge对马克·扎克伯格(Mark Zuckerberg)的完整采访，了解他是如何做出这一决定的，他想要制造什么工具，以及他是否计划在冠状病毒威胁消退后重返办公室。
note_en:
Skip to main content                                                                                                                In a move that illustrates how swiftly the COVID-19 pandemic is reshaping the global economy, Facebook said today that it would begin allowing most of its employees to request a permanent change in their jobs to let them work remotely. The company will begin today by making most of its US job openings eligible for remote hires and begin taking applications for permanent remote work among its workforce later this year.
 “We’re going to be the most forward-leaning company on remote work at our scale,” CEO Mark Zuckerberg said in an interview with  The Verge. “We need to do this in a way that’s thoughtful and responsible, so we’re going to do this in a measured way. But I think that it’s possible that over the next five to 10 years — maybe closer to 10 than five, but somewhere in that range — I think we could get to about half of the company working remotely permanently.”
 Facebook, which has more than 48,000 employees working in 70 offices worldwide, is the largest company yet to move aggressively into remote work in the wake of the pandemic. Twitter  announced last week it would give most of its workforce the option of working remotely, and  Coinbase followed with a similar announcement on Wednesday. Shopify CEO Tobi Lütke  said Thursday that it would immediately begin a shift to permanent remote work. Google CEO Sundar Pichai  told  The Verge this week that the company is considering additional remote work flexibility beyond letting most employees stay home through the end of the year.
  Collectively, the embrace of remote work upends decades of conventional wisdom in Silicon Valley, where the largest companies have been built on the idea of collaboration in close physical proximity. Until recently, Facebook paid new employees  a bonus of up to $15,000 if they agreed to live within 10 miles of the office. Now many of them will be able to work wherever they like — although Facebook will reduce the pay of workers who move to less expensive areas.
 In the near term, Facebook’s shift to remote work has been borne out of necessity. When the company begins reopening some of its offices July 6th, it plans to reduce occupancy to 25 percent of normal,  the company said this week. And the added safety requirements for coming into the office, which include mandatory masks and temperature checks, are likely to keep many workers away for much longer.
 But after surveying employees and talking with executives at other companies built on remote employees, Zuckerberg said he became persuaded of the benefits of a more distributed workforce. The move will open up Facebook jobs to a much wider pool of applicants, he said, and could have a positive effect on the environment. (Among other reasons, remote work could enable thousands of Facebook employees to abandon their daily hours-long commutes to and from its headquarters in Menlo Park.)
 The move also forces Facebook, which is investing billions in creating next-generation tools for communication, to rely on those tools to get work done. In that sense, the move to remote work represents a high-stakes initiative to get Facebook employees to “ dogfood” new products involving augmented reality, virtual reality, the company’s Portal smart display, and its Workplace collaboration tool — and to improve them more rapidly out of necessity, since the company is relying on them to get work done.
 “You have to believe in some capacity that you’re helping people be able to do whatever they want from wherever they are.”
 “VR and AR is all about giving people remote presence,” Zuckerberg said. “So if you’re you’re long on VR and AR and on video chat, you have to believe in some capacity that you’re helping people be able to do whatever they want from wherever they are. So I think that that suggests a worldview that would lead to allowing people to work more remotely over time.”
 But the move also comes with risks. Since March, employees at Facebook and other large tech companies have been working on plans that were set in motion by workers collaborating together on campus. It remains an open question whether companies can be as creative or productive in the long term in environments where they aren’t brainstorming around the same conference table. (Some would say that Facebook  has not been particularly creative in recent years, anyway.)
 Zuckerberg said he expects to move slowly toward remote work, but would begin by making more of the company’s open roles available to remote workers. Others will likely continue to prefer working at a company office — particularly younger employees, Zuckerberg said.
 Another effort to preserve team camaraderie will come from regularly inviting employees onto campus for “onsites,” Zuckerberg said — the on-campus version of the offsite retreats that were common in the business world until the pandemic hit. In fact, he said, travel costs associated with onsite meetings would likely cancel out many of the savings the company might otherwise expect from a reduced real estate footprint.
 “The tools we have today are fairly transactional — that’s a weakness in the ecosystem.”
 Still, many questions about the company’s shift to remote work remain. Facebook used to lure new employees with a campus designed by Frank Gehry and lavish perks; what will it offer employees who are working from home? How will a mostly remote workforce affect efforts around diversity and inclusion? How will younger employees find mentorship in a world where they rarely interact with their mentors in person?
  “I think the tools we have today are fairly transactional — that’s a weakness in the ecosystem,” he said. “Email and messaging — these things are designed for exchanging thoughts, but not hanging out in. It’s almost like what you really want is to have a team just spend unstructured time together.”
 Zuckerberg has shifted from inviting top managers over for monthly dinners at his house to hanging out using  the company’s new Rooms product, he said. He’s also experimenting with holding management meetings in VR.
 Assuming that the COVID-19 risk eventually subsides, Zuckerberg plans to make at least a partial return to the office. The role of CEO has historically required travel and in-person meetings that might not be possible to do online. But Zuckerberg said that he, too, would likely continue to work from home more than he did before the pandemic.
 “I do think I’ll plan to spend more of my time remotely over time,” he said.
 Read The Verge ’s full interview with Mark Zuckerberg  about how he made this decision, what tools he wants to build, and whether he plans to return to the office when the COVID-19 threat subsides.
title: Facebook表示，将永久性地将数万个工作岗位转移到远程工作
title_en: Facebook says it will permanently shift tens of thousands of jobs to remote work

note_en:
Apple released iOS 13.5 and iPadOS 13.5 for iPhones, iPods, and iPads today. They went live alongside minor software updates for Apple TV and HomePod devices. The iOS update mainly adds new health-related features—most notably the  much-discussed Exposure Notification API that was co-developed with Google to help local, regional, and national governments enact contact-tracing strategies to battle the COVID-19 pandemic.
 Usage of the contact tracing API is user-optional. Additionally, exposure reports require the user to, once again, opt in to the notification, and it can only be done after the positive result is confirmed by one of the government agencies.
 Additionally, iPhone and iPad users who rely on Face ID to access content or services on their devices will find a new, simplified process for logging in if they are wearing protective facemasks that interfere with the face-scanning technology,  as previously reported.
 Users of all supported devices will now be able to change Group FaceTime call settings so that, if a participant is speaking at a given moment, they will no longer cause video tiles to automatically change size. iPhone users in the United States can now choose to share some health information stored on their phones automatically with emergency services when calling emergency services.
     Finally, there are a few bug fixes in the mix. Both iPhones and iPads got a fix for "an issue in the share sheet where suggestions and actions may not load" and "an issue where users may see a black screen when trying to play streaming video from some websites," according to the update notes. Additionally, iPadOS users are getting resolutions for various security issues, Apple says, "including some that could lead to unexpected memory corruption or stability issues with the Mail app."
 Apple also updated tvOS and the HomePod software to version 13.4.5 today (no major new features are listed), and it released iOS 12.4.7 for devices that are not supported by iOS 13. The company pushed out a minor watchOS update for the Apple Watch earlier this week; that update (watchOS 6.2.5) brought certain health-related notifications to users in Saudi Arabia, and it added new rainbow-colored Pride watch faces. Today also saw the seed of the fifth beta of macOS Catalina 10.15.5—an update for Macs that will likely reach the general public before too long.
